content.text: is the runtime layer that loads model weights into memory and executes the forward pass that converts a prompt into tokens. Two engines dominate local LLM deployment:
, a high-throughput GPU server built for production API serving, and

, a C++ implementation that runs quantized models on CPUs, Apple Silicon, and commodity GPUs without requiring a data-centre setup. Understanding how each engine works — and when to use which — is the first engineering decision in any local-model deployment.

Every large language model is a function that maps a sequence of integer token IDs to a probability distribution over the next token. An inference engine wraps that function with everything required to make it production-useful:
so multiple callers share GPU time efficiently,
to turn logit vectors into discrete tokens, and

to emit tokens to the caller as they are produced rather than waiting for the complete response. Without an engine, a bare PyTorch model handles one request at a time at low utilization; with the right engine, the same GPU can serve hundreds of concurrent users.

, a memory management scheme that treats GPU VRAM like an OS virtual memory system — the KV cache is divided into fixed-size pages and allocated non-contiguously, eliminating the fragmentation that wasted 60–80 % of GPU memory in earlier serving systems. llama.cpp (Georgi Gerganov, 2023) compiles transformer forward passes into hand-tuned C++ operations on top of the GGML tensor library, with first-class support for GGUF quantization formats that reduce a 70B parameter model to 40 GB — small enough for a high-end workstation.

The inference engine is the primary determinant of how much a local LLM deployment costs, how many concurrent users it can serve, and whether it can operate without internet access. A naively loaded Hugging Face model runs at 10–20 % GPU utilisation under concurrent load because of KV-cache fragmentation and absence of batching. vLLM's continuous batching and PagedAttention close that gap dramatically — production benchmarks report 3–10x higher throughput on identical hardware compared to Transformers serving. For agentic workloads where an LLM is called dozens of times per task, that multiplier translates directly into cost and latency.

On the edge and privacy side, llama.cpp makes it practical to run capable models entirely on-device. A healthcare agent that cannot send patient notes outside the hospital, a legal assistant constrained by data residency rules, or a field-deployed system without reliable connectivity all benefit from local inference with zero data egress. llama.cpp runs a 3B model at 60–80 tokens/second on a modern MacBook Pro (M-series, Metal) and a 7B model at 20–30 tokens/second on a mid-range CPU.
: prompts and completions stay within your infrastructure. No vendor has access to inputs or outputs.
: hardware cost is fixed. Running one million requests costs the same per token whether it is 10 or 10 000 concurrent users (up to hardware ceiling).
: you own the request queue. No shared-infrastructure congestion, rate-limit jitter, or cold-start from an external provider.
: deploy fine-tuned variants, merged LoRA adapters, or unreleased research checkpoints that no public API offers.
: llama.cpp works completely air-gapped. Critical for field robots, medical devices, or classified environments.
Python library works without modification; just change
to point at the local server.
: GGUF Q4 formats reduce a 7B model from ~14 GB (FP16) to ~4 GB, making it runnable on consumer hardware with 8 GB RAM.
: vLLM requires a CUDA GPU with enough VRAM to hold all model weights in FP16 or FP8. A 70B model needs ~140 GB VRAM — typically two A100 80 GB GPUs. Not viable on a laptop.

: Q4_K_M quantization reduces model memory by ~4x but can lower benchmark accuracy by 1–5 points on reasoning tasks. For many chat applications the difference is imperceptible; for coding or math it may matter.
: you own model updates, GPU driver management, container deployments, and capacity planning — all hidden by managed APIs.
: loading a 70B model into GPU memory takes 30–120 seconds. The engine must stay resident between requests; serverless cold-start patterns do not apply.
: CPU inference with llama.cpp produces 5–30 tokens/second for 7B models. Suitable for interactive single-user use but not for batch document processing or high-concurrency APIs.
: model weights must be in the correct format. vLLM prefers Hugging Face FP16 safetensors; llama.cpp requires GGUF. Converting between formats adds a setup step.
Every inference engine executes the same four-stage pipeline. Understanding each stage tells you where latency originates and which knobs to tune.
The raw prompt string is converted to a sequence of integer token IDs using the model's vocabulary tokenizer (stored in
). Most modern LLMs use Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE). A 1000-word prompt is typically 1200–1500 tokens. Tokenization runs on CPU and adds 1–5 ms — rarely the bottleneck.

The full prompt token sequence is fed through all transformer layers in a single batched forward pass. Each layer computes query, key, and value projections; the attention matrix is computed and the resulting key/value tensors are stored in the
for reuse during generation. Prefill is

and scales linearly with prompt length. A 4096-token context on an A100 80 GB takes approximately 200–500 ms depending on model size. Systems with many short prompts spend most of their time in prefill.

After prefill, tokens are generated one at a time. Each decode step runs only the single new token through the model, loading the previously cached KV pairs from VRAM to avoid recomputing attention over the full context. The final transformer layer outputs a logit vector of length
(vocabulary size, typically 32 000–128 000). A sampling strategy converts logits to a probability distribution and draws the next token. Decode is

: each step reads all model weights from VRAM regardless of batch size. vLLM's continuous batching runs many sequences through the decode step simultaneously, amortizing that bandwidth cost across requests.
of the logit vector. Temperature sampling scales logits by

before softmax; lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the most likely token. Top-p (nucleus) sampling restricts the vocabulary to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds

. After a token is sampled, the engine checks stop conditions: EOS token encountered, max-length reached, or a user-specified stop string matched. When generation ends, the token ID sequence is converted back to a UTF-8 string by the tokenizer's decode path.

attrs.alt: Inference Engine Architecture diagram showing vLLM and llama.cpp parallel pipelines from request to output tokens

content.text: Incoming HTTP requests arrive at a FastAPI server exposing the OpenAI Chat Completions API. The
that implements continuous batching: as soon as one sequence finishes and its KV pages are freed, a waiting request is admitted — no gap between batches. The

implements PagedAttention: each sequence's KV cache is stored in fixed-size blocks (pages) of 16 or 32 tokens. Blocks are reference-counted; sequences sharing a prefix (e.g., the same system prompt) share physical blocks with copy-on-write semantics, saving VRAM. CUDA kernels (including flash-attention variants) perform the actual matrix multiplications.
llama.cpp loads the GGUF file using

, so only the pages actually read during inference are faulted into RAM. The GGML computation graph is built once at load time and re-used for every forward pass. Quantized weights (e.g., Q4_K_M) are stored in a packed 4-bit format and dequantized on-the-fly during GEMM operations using SIMD intrinsics (AVX2 on x86, NEON on ARM). On Apple Silicon, the Metal backend offloads matrix multiplications to the GPU unified memory pool; the CUDA backend does the same for NVIDIA GPUs. The Python binding
wraps the C library via ctypes and exposes both a low-level

: you have one or more NVIDIA GPUs with sufficient VRAM, need to serve multiple concurrent users (APIs, agent orchestrators, batch jobs), and want OpenAI-compatible endpoints with minimal code changes.
: you need to run on CPU, Apple Silicon, or a single consumer GPU; data must stay on-device; or you are building a local assistant, desktop app, or embedded tool.
Use llama.cpp with GPU offload when
: you have a GPU that is smaller than the full model in FP16 but large enough to hold many layers in Q4 — partial offload (
) dramatically increases throughput vs pure CPU.
Use vLLM with tensor parallelism when
: a single GPU cannot fit the model; vLLM shards layers across multiple GPUs transparently with
: you need frontier model quality (GPT-4 class), your team lacks GPU ops expertise, or you serve sporadic traffic where managed API cold-start is not a problem.
Start the server from the command line, then use the standard
library to call it. No changes to existing agent code are required beyond updating

# Step 1 — install vLLM (requires CUDA 11.8+ and a GPU with sufficient VRAM)
# pip install vllm

# ─── Launch the server (shell command) ───────────────────────────────────────
# python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
#   --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
#   --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
#   --max-model-len 8192 \
#   --dtype bfloat16 \
#   --port 8000
#
# Flags explained:
#   --gpu-memory-utilization  fraction of VRAM to use for KV cache (leave headroom)
#   --max-model-len           maximum context window (tokens); trim to fit VRAM
#   --dtype                   bfloat16 is stable on Ampere+; float16 on older GPUs
#   --tensor-parallel-size 2  uncomment to split across 2 GPUs (e.g., 70B models)

# ─── Python client (works with any OpenAI-compatible engine) ─────────────────
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="ignored",           # vLLM accepts any non-empty string
)

def chat(prompt: str, system: str = "You are a helpful assistant.") -> str:
    """Single-turn chat with local vLLM server, streaming to stdout."""
    stream = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": system},
            {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
        ],
        temperature=0.2,
        max_tokens=512,
        stream=True,
    )
    output = []
    for chunk in stream:
        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
        print(delta, end="", flush=True)
        output.append(delta)
    print()
    return "".join(output)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    answer = chat("Explain the difference between prefill and decode in LLM inference.")
    print(f"\nTokens generated: {len(answer.split())}")
, download a GGUF weight file from Hugging Face, and load it directly in Python. The Metal/CUDA backend is enabled at install time via a CMake flag.

# Install the Python bindings (no GPU — CPU-only build)
# pip install llama-cpp-python
#
# For Apple Silicon (Metal GPU acceleration):
# CMAKE_ARGS="-DLLAMA_METAL=on" pip install llama-cpp-python
#
# For CUDA:
# CMAKE_ARGS="-DLLAMA_CUDA=on" pip install llama-cpp-python
#
# Download a GGUF model (example: Llama-3.2-3B-Q4_K_M):
# wget https://huggingface.co/bartowski/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-GGUF/resolve/main/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf

from llama_cpp import Llama

# Load model — uses memory-mapped file; no full copy to RAM until pages are touched
llm = Llama(
    model_path="Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf",
    n_ctx=4096,          # context window in tokens
    n_threads=8,         # CPU threads for matrix operations
    n_gpu_layers=0,      # set to -1 (all layers) for Metal/CUDA offload
    verbose=False,
)

def generate(prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 256) -> str:
    """Chat-completion style call using the ChatML template."""
    response = llm.create_chat_completion(
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise technical assistant."},
            {"role": "user",   "content": prompt},
        ],
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=0.1,
        top_p=0.9,
        stream=False,
    )
    return response["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]

# Streaming version — useful for interactive terminals
def generate_stream(prompt: str):
    for chunk in llm.create_chat_completion(
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=512,
        stream=True,
    ):
        delta = chunk["choices"][0]["delta"].get("content", "")
        if delta:
            print(delta, end="", flush=True)
    print()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(generate("What is PagedAttention?"))
    print("\n--- streaming ---")
    generate_stream("List three advantages of GGUF quantization.")
Always measure tokens-per-second on your specific hardware and model before committing to a configuration. The following script provides a repeatable baseline.

"""
Benchmark script: measure tokens-per-second for vLLM and llama-cpp-python.
Run once to baseline, then tune knobs (batch size, quantization, GPU layers).
"""
import time
import statistics
from openai import OpenAI
from llama_cpp import Llama

PROMPT = "Summarize the key trade-offs between CPU and GPU inference for large language models."
REPEATS = 5

# ─── vLLM benchmark ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def bench_vllm(model_id: str, n_tokens: int = 200) -> float:
    client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="x")
    times = []
    for _ in range(REPEATS):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        resp = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model_id,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
            max_tokens=n_tokens,
            temperature=0.0,
            stream=False,
        )
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        generated = resp.usage.completion_tokens
        times.append(generated / elapsed)
    tps = statistics.mean(times)
    print(f"vLLM  {model_id}: {tps:.1f} tok/s (mean over {REPEATS} runs)")
    return tps

# ─── llama.cpp benchmark ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def bench_llamacpp(model_path: str, n_gpu_layers: int = 0) -> float:
    llm = Llama(model_path=model_path, n_ctx=2048,
                n_gpu_layers=n_gpu_layers, verbose=False)
    times = []
    for _ in range(REPEATS):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        resp = llm.create_chat_completion(
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
            max_tokens=200, temperature=0.0, stream=False,
        )
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        generated = resp["usage"]["completion_tokens"]
        times.append(generated / elapsed)
    tps = statistics.mean(times)
    label = "GPU" if n_gpu_layers else "CPU"
    print(f"llama.cpp ({label}): {tps:.1f} tok/s (mean over {REPEATS} runs)")
    return tps

# Typical results on common hardware:
# vLLM (A100 80GB, Llama-3.1-8B):  ~1800 tok/s with batch_size=32
# vLLM (RTX 4090, Llama-3.1-8B):   ~350  tok/s single request
# llama.cpp CPU (Ryzen 9, Q4_K_M): ~25   tok/s
# llama.cpp Metal (M3 Pro, Q4_K_M): ~70  tok/s
An agent using LangChain or LlamaIndex calls the LLM via

. Tool calls, streaming, and parallel function execution all work exactly as with the hosted API. The only agent-visible difference is slightly longer first-token latency on the first request (model warm-up) and much lower per-token cost at scale.

A retrieval-augmented generation pipeline uses a small llama.cpp model (3B–7B) as the reader LLM. Documents are chunked, embedded with a small embedding model (also runnable via llama.cpp), stored in a local vector store (Chroma, FAISS), and retrieved at query time. The complete pipeline runs on a MacBook Pro with no internet access — the only disk reads are the GGUF weight file and the vector index.
Practical GGUF quantization choices for a 7B parameter model:

A healthcare startup builds an AI assistant that summarizes clinical notes and suggests ICD-10 codes. Patient data cannot leave the hospital's network. The team deploys vLLM on a pair of A100 40 GB GPUs running
with tensor parallelism across both GPUs. The vLLM server serves the hospital's internal network over the OpenAI-compatible API. Existing Python code using

. Average first-token latency is 800 ms (dominated by a 4096-token prefill); subsequent tokens stream at ~95 tokens/second. GPU utilisation stays above 85 % during peak clinical hours because vLLM's continuous batching efficiently interleaves thirty to fifty concurrent note-summarisation requests.
For the clinic's mobile diagnostic tablet running offline in rural clinics, the team builds a lightweight companion app using

via llama.cpp. The model fits in 2 GB of RAM and generates at 55 tokens/second on the tablet's ARM CPU. It handles triage questions and symptom checking without any network access, syncing summaries to the hospital system when connectivity is restored.
: the default allocates KV cache for the model's full context (e.g., 128 K tokens for Llama-3). If your prompts are shorter, use
to free the unused VRAM for more concurrent requests.
Setting n_ctx too large in llama.cpp
: the KV cache is pre-allocated in RAM at load time.
on a model with 7B parameters allocates ~2 GB of RAM for cache alone. Set
to the actual maximum prompt + completion length you expect.
Choosing Q2 quantization to save RAM

: Q2_K cuts file size by another 1 GB vs Q3 but causes perceptible quality degradation on instruction-following and reasoning tasks. Q4_K_M is the empirically validated sweet spot; only go lower as a last resort.
: the default uses all available CPU threads, which can cause thrashing on shared servers. Benchmark with 4, 8, and 16 threads; the optimal value is usually physical core count (not hyperthreads).
Leaving gpu-memory-utilization at 0.90 for small models
: if the model only uses 20 GB on a 40 GB GPU, setting utilisation to 0.50 frees the rest for other containers. Conversely, leaving it at 0.90 when the model barely fits causes OOM on large batches.
: FP32 doubles VRAM relative to FP16 for no quality gain. Always use
in vLLM. Bfloat16 is preferred on Ampere (A100) and newer; float16 on Volta (V100) and older.
. Writing code to this interface makes it trivial to swap engines or fall back to the hosted API.
: measure prefill time, decode throughput, and GPU utilisation under realistic concurrent load using the bench script above. Most optimisations are workload-specific.
Separate the embedding model from the generation model
: use a small, fast embedding model (e.g., nomic-embed-text via llama.cpp) for RAG retrieval and a larger generation model for reasoning. Do not call the generation model for embedding.
Monitor KV cache usage in vLLM
; sustained >95% means KV cache is the bottleneck and you should reduce
Use guided decoding for structured output
in the request body. This forces the model to produce valid JSON for tool-use pipelines without post-processing.
Run llama.cpp with mlock on production servers
to prevent the OS from swapping out model pages during idle periods, which would cause the next request to block on I/O.

vLLM throughput scales near-linearly with GPU count via tensor parallelism up to the point where inter-GPU communication overhead dominates (typically beyond 8 GPUs for a single model). For workloads beyond a single server, multiple vLLM instances can be load-balanced behind a standard HTTP reverse proxy (nginx, Envoy) using round-robin or least-connections routing.
splits each layer's weight matrices across N GPUs. Requires NVLink for optimal bandwidth; PCIe works but reduces gains.
assigns consecutive transformer layers to different GPUs. Useful when NVLink is unavailable.
: vLLM can cache KV pages for common prefixes (e.g., long system prompts). Requests sharing the same prefix skip prefill for those tokens, reducing first-token latency by 30–70 %.

: a small draft model generates K tokens in one step; the larger model verifies them in parallel. Correctly predicted tokens are accepted for free, increasing effective throughput when output is predictable (e.g., code completions).
: set to -1 to offload all layers to GPU (Metal/CUDA). Partial offload (e.g., 20 of 32 layers) provides a large speed boost on GPUs smaller than the full model.
: the prefill batch size (tokens processed together). Increasing from the default 512 to 2048 improves prefill speed at the cost of RAM. Match to your typical prompt length.
: if the GGUF model supports extended context via RoPE scaling, increase this value to unlock longer contexts without fine-tuning.
One-command local model management; wraps llama.cpp
GUI for downloading and running GGUF models
Chat UI that connects to OpenAI-compatible APIs
Alternative Python bindings (older GGUF predecessor GGML)
Bind only to localhost by default
: vLLM and llama-cpp-python's HTTP server listen on
by default. In production, bind to
and place a TLS-terminating reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) in front. Never expose the raw engine port to the public internet.

: local models lack the safety fine-tuning of managed APIs. User-supplied content injected directly into system prompts can override model instructions. Separate untrusted user input from trusted system context using structural delimiters and treat all model outputs as untrusted.
: without per-request token limits, a malicious caller can exhaust GPU memory by submitting maximum-length prompts. Set
caps and enforce them at the API gateway layer.
: GPU OOM kills the engine process silently on some configurations. Run the engine under a process supervisor (systemd, Docker restart policy) and alert on unexpected restarts.

: llama.cpp's GGUF format evolves; a weight file loaded with an older binary may silently produce wrong outputs without an error. Pin both the binary version and the GGUF conversion tool version in your deployment artifacts.
Inference engines bridge raw model weights and real-world usage by adding KV-cache management, batching, sampling, and streaming.
vLLM's PagedAttention eliminates KV-cache fragmentation and enables continuous batching — the two changes responsible for the majority of its throughput advantage.
llama.cpp enables practical LLM inference on CPUs and Apple Silicon via GGUF quantization, at the cost of reduced throughput and some quality loss at aggressive quantization levels.
Both engines expose OpenAI-compatible HTTP APIs, making it straightforward to swap between local and cloud providers with minimal code changes.
Choose vLLM for multi-user GPU servers and production API endpoints; choose llama.cpp for privacy-first, on-device, or resource-constrained deployments.
Always measure actual tokens-per-second on your hardware and prompt distribution before optimising — theoretical throughput numbers rarely match real workloads.
Think of an inference engine as an operating system for model weights.

The model file is a program binary. The inference engine is the kernel: it manages hardware resources (VRAM, CPU threads), schedules concurrent workloads (batching), handles memory allocation (KV cache pages), and exposes a stable API to applications. Just as an OS lets you run many processes on one CPU without rewriting every application, an inference engine lets you serve many concurrent LLM requests on one GPU without modifying the model. vLLM is a high-end server OS; llama.cpp is a lean embedded OS — same abstraction, different hardware target.
